runs.interpretation: Primary-earner substitution-response elasticity for earnings decile 2 in a U.S. microsimulation model
citations: Saez, Slemrod, and Giertz (2012) on taxable income elasticities and labor-supply margins
Eissa and Liebman (1996) / EITC labor-supply evidence for primary vs. secondary earners

runs.reasoningSummary: My memory-level prior is that primary earners tend to have modest intensive-margin substitution elasticities, generally below those often assumed for secondary earners. For a low-earning but employed primary-earner group such as decile 2, I place the center in the low-to-mid range around 0.15, with meaningful uncertainty because tax-benefit microsimulation definitions and bounded net-of-tax wage changes can shift the mapped coefficient.

runs.reasoningSummary: My baseline memory is that primary earners generally have modest substitution elasticities, lower than many secondary earners, but decile-2 workers may be somewhat more responsive than high earners because hours/participation margins are less fixed. In a current-law U.S. tax-benefit microsimulation using bounded relative changes in effective marginal wage, I therefore place the central value in the low-to-mid range around 0.15, with substantial uncertainty but still usually below about 0.4.
